skill.name: mmx-cli
skill.description: Use mmx to create text, images, videos, speech, music, web search results, and MiniMax resource outputs from the terminal.
skill.summary: Run MiniMax multimodal generation and account commands through the mmx CLI.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many high-risk patterns, but most are Markdown command examples and model names rather than executable skill code. Real risks remain because the skill instructs agents to run an external CLI, install a global npm package, persist API credentials, read local files, write generated media, and send data to MiniMax services.

medium_findings.title: Persistent API Credential Handling
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The guide tells users to authenticate with an API key, persist credentials under a hidden home directory path, pass keys on the command line, and export MINIMAX_API_KEY. This is legitimate for an API CLI, but it creates secret exposure risk through config files, shell history, logs, and inherited environments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly document credential persistence and environment variable use. The behavior is expected for this CLI, so the finding is medium rather than high.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution and Global Package Install
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs agents to install mmx-cli globally with npm and execute many mmx commands. The examples are documentation, not hidden malware, but users delegate shell execution to an external package and remote API client.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible Markdown examples and match the skill purpose. Risk remains because execution depends on a separately installed npm package.

medium_findings.title: Local File Read and Write Paths
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: Several commands accept local paths, stdin, output directories, downloads, and raw streams. This can read user files into MiniMax services or write generated files locally when an agent runs the commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file path flags are explicit and repeated across commands. The usage is legitimate, but it can expose local content if prompts or paths are not reviewed.

medium_findings.title: Remote Service and Callback Data Flow
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill is built around MiniMax cloud calls, web search, media generation, and optional callback URLs. User prompts, files, queries, and task metadata can leave the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is central to the documented functionality. No malicious endpoint is shown, but publication should warn about external data transfer.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto and Obfuscation Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The static weak-cryptography, Unicode escape, and system reconnaissance alerts point to Markdown tables, model identifiers, a literal Unicode lyric value, /dev/null redirection, and shell examples. I found no evidence of cryptographic code, obfuscated execution, or reconnaissance logic in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is visible documentation, not executable code embedded in the skill. The scanner likely matched short tokens, escapes, and shell syntax without semantic context.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Check Found No Evidence
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: I searched for override phrases, fake system messages, pre-approval claims, and instructions to skip review. No evidence found in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill header and guide describe CLI usage without attempting to change the evaluator role. A targeted search found no suspicious prompt-injection phrases.

dangerous_patterns.title: API Key Passed Through CLI and Environment
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide documents API key login, per-call API key use, and MINIMAX_API_KEY export. These patterns can leak secrets through process lists, shell history, logs, or inherited child environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive key handling examples are explicit. They are placeholders, but the workflow would involve real user secrets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in Chained Workflows
dangerous_patterns.description: The piping examples use command substitution to feed generated URLs and task IDs into later commands. This is a normal shell pattern, but outputs should be treated as data and quoted carefully.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples quote variables and use expected CLI output. Risk is moderate-low because command substitution is visible and not applied to arbitrary code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hidden Home Directory Configuration Files
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide references hidden home directory credential and config files. This is expected CLI behavior, but it creates a local persistence point for secrets and account settings.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hidden paths are documented directly. The concern is storage hygiene, not evidence of unauthorized file access.

content.user_title: Generate MiniMax Media from the CLI
content.value_statement: Multimodal generation often requires separate tools for text, images, video, speech, music, and search. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a concise guide for using the mmx terminal workflow.
actual_capabilities: Runs MiniMax text chat commands with messages, system prompts, models, and JSON output.
Generates images and saves result URLs or files through mmx image commands.
Creates video tasks, checks task status, and downloads completed videos.
Synthesizes speech and music with voice, format, timing, and output options.
Describes images and performs MiniMax web search from terminal commands.
Exports mmx command schemas for agent tool registration.
limitations: Requires mmx-cli installation and a valid MiniMax API key before use.
Sends prompts, files, and queries to MiniMax cloud services.
Video generation can be asynchronous and may require polling or later download.
The skill documents commands but does not include the mmx implementation source.

use_cases.title: Produce Campaign Media Assets
use_cases.description: Generate draft images, videos, narration, and background music from structured prompts in one terminal workflow.

use_cases.title: Automate Multimodal Pipelines
use_cases.description: Use JSON output, quiet mode, task polling, and schema export to connect MiniMax commands to agent workflows.

use_cases.title: Review Visual Inputs
use_cases.description: Describe local or uploaded images and combine results with MiniMax search queries for quick investigation.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use mmx to generate one image for this concept: [describe concept]. Return the saved path or image URL.

prompt_templates.title: Draft Voiceover Audio
prompt_templates.prompt: Use mmx speech synthesis for this script: [paste script]. Save an MP3 file and use a clear narrator voice.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Async Video Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Start an async MiniMax video task for this prompt: [video prompt]. Then show me how to check status and download it.

prompt_templates.title: Export Agent Tool Schemas
prompt_templates.prompt: Export the mmx tool schema for the commands needed by this workflow: [workflow]. Explain which commands should be registered.

output_examples.input: Generate an image of a product concept and save it locally.
output_examples.output: The assistant runs the image generation command, uses quiet output, and reports the saved image path or generated image URL.

output_examples.input: Create narration for a short announcement.
output_examples.output: The assistant synthesizes the provided text with the requested voice settings and returns the audio file path.

output_examples.input: Start a video generation task without waiting.
output_examples.output: The assistant starts the async task, records the task ID, and explains the next command to check progress.

best_practices: Use non-interactive, quiet, and JSON output flags when agents run commands.
Prefer environment variables or secure credential storage over passing real API keys in prompts.
Review local file paths before commands upload inputs or write generated media.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real API keys into shared prompts, logs, or command transcripts.
Do not run media generation on private files without confirming the intended upload.
Do not rely on async video output without saving the task ID for later checks.

faq.question: What does this skill help an AI agent do?
faq.answer: It guides the agent through mmx commands for MiniMax text, image, video, speech, music, vision, search, and quota tasks.

faq.question: Does it require an API key?
faq.answer: Yes. The mmx CLI requires MiniMax authentication through login, per-call flags, or environment variables.

faq.question: Can it save generated media files?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented commands support output directories, download paths, audio files, and streamed media output.

faq.question: Can it be used in automated agent workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. The guide recommends non-interactive mode, quiet output, JSON output, dry runs, and schema export.

faq.question: What data may leave the local machine?
faq.answer: Prompts, search queries, selected local files, media inputs, callback metadata, and API credentials may be sent to MiniMax services.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is suitable for publication with warnings about external command execution, cloud data transfer, and credential handling.
